papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.authors: Zhibo Chu, Zichong Wang, Wenbin Zhang
papers.venue: ACM SIGKDD Explorations Newsletter, vol. 26(1), pp. 34-48
papers.overlap: DIRECT PARENT LINEAGE. Taxonomizes bias in LLMs that answer — covers intrinsic/extrinsic bias, fairness definitions (group, individual, counterfactual), and debiasing of LLM generation. Senior author Wenbin Zhang is also our senior author. Our paper explicitly positions itself as 'the agentic successor' to this work.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chu et al. treat LLMs as static generators; our survey treats LLMs as components of acting pipelines (tool selection, memory, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift). Their measurement axis is token-level output; ours is action-level disparity. We inherit their definitional grounding and extend every dimension to the agentic surface they do not treat.

papers.title: A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.authors: Ninareh Mehrabi, Fred Morstatter, Naveen Saxena, Kristina Lerman, Aram Galstyan
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 54, no. 6, pp. 1-35
papers.overlap: Foundational fairness survey cataloguing 20+ bias sources in classical ML pipeline (pre/in/post-processing). Our §2.1 explicitly uses their taxonomy as the 'baseline vocabulary' and extends it upstream into agent components. Widely cited by CSUR reviewers as the canonical ML fairness reference.
papers.how_ours_differs: Mehrabi et al. cover classical ML pipelines only, with no LLM or agent coverage. We extend their taxonomy to the six agent-pipeline components that did not exist when they wrote. The reviewer expectation is engagement, which we provide explicitly in §2.1.

papers.title: Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey
papers.authors: Simon Caton, Christian Haas
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 56, no. 7, Article 144
papers.overlap: CSUR survey organising ML fairness into pre/in/post-processing. Covers group fairness, individual fairness, and mitigation. No LLM or agent content. Likely cited by CSUR reviewers as adjacent CSUR prior art our survey should engage.
papers.how_ours_differs: Caton and Haas scope to classical supervised learning. Ours is entirely focused on LLM-based agents and the new fairness failure modes (tool routing, RAG injection, role assignment, long-horizon drift) that their pre/in/post taxonomy cannot capture. We cite them for definitional grounding only.

papers.title: A Review on Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.authors: Dana Pessach, Eytan Shmueli
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 3, Article 51
papers.overlap: Another CSUR fairness-in-ML survey covering causes of algorithmic bias and common group/individual fairness measures, focusing on classification tasks. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with this survey as prior CSUR art.
papers.how_ours_differs: Pessach and Shmueli scope to classification and prediction, not to generation or agency. The agent-pipeline loci we taxonomize (tool selection, multi-agent delegation, etc.) are entirely outside their scope.

papers.title: What-is and How-to for Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey, Reflection, and Perspective
papers.authors: Zeyu Tang, Jiji Zhang, Kun Zhang
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 13s, Article 299
papers.overlap: CSUR survey linking ML fairness notions to moral/political philosophy (theories of justice) and addressing long-term impact of decisions. The long-term fairness angle touches our §3.6 on long-horizon drift.
papers.how_ours_differs: Tang et al. discuss long-term impact abstractly in a classical ML framing. We operationalize long-term fairness in LLM agents empirically — multi-turn degradation (FairMT-Bench), sycophancy drift, feedback loops — with agent-specific metrics that their philosophical framing cannot capture.

papers.title: Fairness in Ranking, Part I: Score-Based Ranking
papers.authors: Meike Zehlike, Ke Yang, Julia Stoyanovich
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 6, Article 118
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on fairness in score-based ranking algorithms. Directly relevant to our §3.1 (tool/API selection bias) and §3.2 (retrieval bias), where ranking fairness determines which tools or documents are prioritized. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zehlike et al. treat fairness as a property of a static ranking algorithm. We situate retrieval and tool ranking inside an agent pipeline where the bias compounds across subsequent steps (the agentic multiplier, §3.7). We also treat the ranker as one of six pipeline loci rather than as the entire system.

papers.title: Fairness in Ranking, Part II: Learning-to-Rank and Recommender Systems
papers.authors: Meike Zehlike, Ke Yang, Julia Stoyanovich
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 6, Article 119
papers.overlap: CSUR companion to Part I, extending to learning-to-rank and recommender systems. Directly relevant to §3.5 (user modeling and personalization harms) and our treatment of LLM-based recommenders.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zehlike et al. cover pre-LLM recommender fairness. We cover LLM-native agents that use recommendation logic alongside tool calling, memory, and planning — where personalization harms arise from inferred user models, not explicit recommendation scores. Their metrics do not apply to action-level decisions.

papers.title: A Survey on the Fairness of Recommender Systems
papers.authors: Yifan Wang, Weizhi Ma, Min Zhang, Yiqun Liu, Shaoping Ma
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Information Systems, vol. 41, no. 2, Article 38
papers.overlap: ACM TOIS survey on fairness across consumer, provider, and platform stakeholders in recommender systems. Relevant to §3.5 (personalization harms) and the CFaiRLLM paper we cite.
papers.how_ours_differs: Wang et al. cover classical recommender systems without LLM backbone. We cover LLM-based agents whose 'recommendation' is embedded in a broader action pipeline with tool use, memory, and multi-agent coordination. Their stakeholder framework is useful for consumer fairness (our §3.5) but not for the other five components.

papers.title: Trustworthy AI: From Principles to Practices
papers.authors: Bo Li, Peng Qi, Bo Liu, Shuai Di, Jingen Liu, Jiquan Pei, Jinfeng Yi, Bowen Zhou
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 9, Article 177
papers.overlap: CSUR survey covering the full trustworthy AI stack: robustness, generalization, explainability, transparency, fairness, privacy, and accountability. Fairness is one of seven+ dimensions; no agentic or LLM-agent content. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement as prior CSUR art.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li et al. treat fairness as one dimension among many in a broad trustworthy-AI review covering the full model lifecycle. We take fairness as our sole organizing axis and drill into the agentic pipeline loci that their high-level framework cannot distinguish. They do not cover tool selection, multi-agent delegation, RAG bias, or long-horizon drift.

papers.title: Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence: A Review
papers.authors: Davinder Kaur, Suleyman Uslu, Kaley J. Rittichier, Arjan Durresi
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 2, Article 39
papers.overlap: Earlier CSUR trustworthy AI survey covering fairness, explainability, accountability, reliability. No LLM or agent content. Provides the trustworthy-AI vocabulary that CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kaur et al. predate the LLM agent era and address trustworthiness at the model/system level. Our scope is specifically LLM-based agents and the agent-component loci where fairness fails in ways their framework cannot see.

papers.title: Towards Trustworthy AI: A Review of Ethical and Robust Large Language Models
papers.authors: Md Meftahul Ferdaus, Mahdi Abdelguerfi, Elias Ioup, Kendall N. Niles, Ken Pathak, Steven Sloan
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 7
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on ethical and robust LLMs covering unintended harms (including bias/fairness), transparency, adversarial vulnerability, value alignment, and environmental impact. Covers fairness in LLMs. Does NOT cover agentic systems or agent-pipeline components.
papers.how_ours_differs: Ferdaus et al. review trustworthiness of LLMs as text generators across five ethical dimensions; they do not analyse the agent pipeline. Our distinct contribution is the per-component fairness taxonomy across the six agentic loci (tool selection, memory, delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift) that arise only when LLMs are embedded in acting systems.

papers.title: The Emerged Security and Privacy of LLM Agent: A Survey with Case Studies
papers.authors: Feng He, Tianqing Zhu, Dayong Ye, Bo Liu, Wanlei Zhou, Philip S. Yu
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 6
papers.overlap: CSUR survey specifically on LLM agents, organized around security and privacy threats and defenses across agent components (brain, memory, tool, user, agent, environment). Covers the same six-component agent anatomy we use but through a security/privacy lens.
papers.how_ours_differs: He et al. organize by threat type (privacy leakage, adversarial attacks, jailbreaking) not by fairness. They do not cover demographic disparity, group/individual/counterfactual fairness metrics, or action-level disparity. Our survey is the fairness-axis complement to their security-axis survey — sharing the agent-component anatomy but bringing an entirely different analytical lens and a different body of literature.

papers.title: A Survey on the Optimization of Large Language Model-based Agents
papers.authors: Shangheng Du, Jiabao Zhao, Jinxin Shi, Zhentao Xie, Xin Jiang, Yanhong Bai, Liang He
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 9, Article 223
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on LLM agent optimization covering parameter-driven (fine-tuning, RL) and parameter-free (prompt engineering, retrieval) methods. Agent components covered include planning, memory, tool use. No fairness content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Du et al. optimise for performance (task success, efficiency). We optimise for fairness (demographic parity, counterfactual invariance) — a fundamentally different target that requires different metrics, evaluation protocols, and mitigation strategies. The optimization approaches they review (RL, fine-tuning) appear in our §5 as one mitigation family among several.

papers.title: Generalizability of Large Language Model-Based Agents: A Comprehensive Survey
papers.authors: Minxing Zhang, Yi Yang, Roy Xie, Bhuwan Dhingra, Shuyan Zhou, Jian Pei
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 10, Article 263
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on LLM agent generalizability — consistent performance across varied instructions, tasks, environments, and domains. Categorizes methods targeting the LLM backbone, agent components, or LLM-component interaction. No fairness content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Zhang et al. define generalizability as performance consistency; we define fairness as outcome parity across demographic groups. These are orthogonal goals: a fully generalisable agent can still route protected groups to inferior tools. We cite this CSUR article to show the agent-evaluation space and position our fairness-specific evaluation framework (§4) as complementary.

papers.title: LLLMs: A Data-Driven Survey of Evolving Research on Limitations of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Aida Kostikova, Zhipin Wang, Deidamea Bajri, Ole Putz, Benjamin Paassen, Steffen Eger
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, published April 17, 2026
papers.overlap: CSUR data-driven meta-survey of 14,648 papers on LLM limitations (2022-2025), covering reasoning, generalization, hallucination, bias, and security. Bias/fairness is the fourth most studied limitation category.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kostikova et al. survey LLM limitations at the static-model level with bibliometric methods; their bias category covers QA-era findings. Our survey is scoped exclusively to the agentic surface — the bias failures that emerge only when LLMs are deployed as acting agents — which is not represented as a distinct category in their corpus.

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Yupeng Chang, Xu Wang, Jindong Wang, Yuan Wu, Linyi Yang, Kaijie Zhu, Hao Chen, Xiaoyuan Yi, Cunxiang Wang, Yidong Wang, Wei Ye, Yue Zhang, Yi Chang, Philip S. Yu, Qiang Yang, Xing Xie
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST), vol. 15, no. 3, Article 39
papers.overlap: Major ACM TIST survey on LLM evaluation covering what to evaluate (knowledge, reasoning, fairness, safety), where to evaluate (datasets, benchmarks), and how to evaluate. Fairness is one evaluation dimension. No agentic evaluation framework.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chang et al. evaluate LLMs as text generators; fairness is one of many capabilities to benchmark. Our §4 constructs an evaluation framework specifically for acting agents — group, individual, and counterfactual fairness lifted to trajectory-level outcomes and action-level disparity metrics — that their general survey does not provide.

papers.title: Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey
papers.authors: Mahmoud Mohammadi, Yipeng Li, Jane Lo, Wendy Yip
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 31st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '25)
papers.overlap: NEAR-NEIGHBOR explicitly cited in our positioning (§1.4 and §3). Broad agent-evaluation survey where fairness is one sub-topic among many (task completion, safety, robustness, efficiency). Two-dimensional taxonomy organizes evaluation objectives without making fairness the central axis.
papers.how_ours_differs: Mohammadi et al. treat fairness as one sub-topic in a general agent-evaluation survey. Our survey takes fairness as the sole organizing axis and achieves substantially deeper coverage of each component's bias mechanisms, measurement instruments (CFR, MASD, action-level disparity), and mitigation strategies. This differentiation is explicitly argued in our §1.4 and §3.

papers.title: A Survey on Trustworthy LLM Agents: Threats and Countermeasures
papers.authors: Miao Yu, Fanci Meng, Xinyun Zhou, Shilong Wang, Junyuan Mao, Linsey Pang, Tianlong Chen, Kun Wang, Xinfeng Li, Yongfeng Zhang, Bo An, Qingsong Wen
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 31st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '25)
papers.overlap: Trustworthiness survey of LLM agents organized around intrinsic (brain, memory, tool) and extrinsic (user, agent, environment) aspects. Covers safety, truthfulness, robustness, and some fairness. Uses similar agent-anatomy decomposition to ours.
papers.how_ours_differs: Yu et al. address trustworthiness broadly with fairness as one consideration among many trust properties. We are fairness-dedicated, providing the per-component bias mechanisms, counterfactual evaluation methods (CFR, MASD), and action-level disparity metrics that their general trustworthiness framework does not develop. Their threat taxonomy (adversarial, jailbreak, hallucination) differs from our fairness taxonomy (demographic disparity, compounding bias, personalization harm).

papers.title: Bias and Unfairness in Information Retrieval Systems: New Challenges in the LLM Era
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Chen Xu, Shicheng Xu, Liang Pang, Zhenhua Dong, Jun Xu
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '24)
papers.overlap: Survey and framework for bias and unfairness in LLM-integrated IR systems. Covers how LLM integration introduces new bias challenges at retrieval, ranking, and generation stages. Directly relevant to our §3.2 (memory and retrieval bias). Cited in our draft as 'dai2024bias'.
papers.how_ours_differs: Dai et al. focus on the IR pipeline (query, retrieval, ranking, generation) as an end-to-end system. We situate retrieval as one of six agent components and analyse how retrieval-injected bias compounds with bias from tool selection, multi-agent delegation, and planning — the cross-component compounding analysis is absent from their IR-centric scope.

papers.title: Actions Speak Louder than Words: Agent Decisions Reveal Implicit Biases in Language Models
papers.authors: Yuxuan Li, Hirokazu Shirado, Sauvik Das
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '25)
papers.overlap: FAccT paper showing that agent decision-making reveals implicit biases invisible to QA-level evaluation — the empirical anchor of our measurement-gap thesis. Cited extensively in our draft as 'li2025actions'. Proposes simulating agents with sociodemographic personas and measuring decision disparities.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li et al. demonstrate the measurement gap in one experimental setting (persona-based agent simulations in specific decision scenarios). Our survey generalises this finding across all six agent components, provides the formal action-level disparity metrics (§4.3), and situates the result in the broader taxonomy. We do not compete with this paper — we build the survey framework on top of it.

papers.title: Visibility into AI Agents
papers.authors: Alan Chan, Carson Ezell, Max Kaufmann, Kevin Wei, Lewis Hammond, Herbie Bradley, Emma Bluemke, Nitarshan Rajkumar, David Krueger, Noam Kolt, Lennart Heim, Markus Anderljung
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2024 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '24)
papers.overlap: FAccT position paper arguing that delegation of complex goals to AI agents exacerbates societal risks. Proposes transparency mechanisms: unique agent tags, real-time monitoring, activity logging. Touches accountability infrastructure relevant to our §8 research agenda.
papers.how_ours_differs: Chan et al. address AI agent visibility and governance mechanisms (what information should be disclosed). We address fairness and equity (whether agents produce disparate outcomes across demographic groups) — adjacent but orthogonal concerns. We cite this for the accountability framing in our research agenda.

papers.title: Towards a framework for Bias Evaluation of AI Agents in Agentic Workflows
papers.authors: Unknown (ACM HCAIep '26 proceedings)
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 Conference on Human Centred Artificial Intelligence - Education and Practice (HCAIep '26)
papers.overlap: Presents a bias evaluation framework for multi-agent workflows with demographic swapping methodology and statistical analysis of bias evolution across sequential agent interactions. Directly overlaps our §4 (evaluation) and §3.7 (agentic multiplier).
papers.how_ours_differs: This paper presents one evaluation framework for one workflow setting. Our survey taxonomizes bias across all six agent components, covers the full landscape of evaluation methods (CFR, MASD, action-level disparity, group/individual/counterfactual families), provides a mitigation map, and delivers the comprehensive component-organized taxonomy that this single-paper contribution does not. We should cite and position explicitly relative to it.

papers.title: Software Fairness: An Analysis and Survey
papers.authors: Ezekiel Soremekun, Mike Papadakis, Maxime Cordy, Yves Le Traon
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 3 (October 2025)
papers.overlap: CSUR survey analysing 164 publications on fairness as a software engineering property, covering specification, testing, and mitigation of bias in learning-based software. Mentions intersectional and sequential fairness as open challenges.
papers.how_ours_differs: Soremekun et al. treat fairness as a software property of ML systems in general, with emphasis on testing and verification methods. They do not cover LLM agents, agentic architectures, or the agent-component loci we taxonomize. Their 'sequential fairness' mention is an open challenge in their framework; we address it in depth in §3.6 (long-horizon drift) and §4.

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.authors: Isabel O. Gallegos, Ryan A. Rossi, Joe Barrow, Md Mehrab Tanjim, Sungchul Kim, Franck Dernoncourt, Tong Yu, Ruiyi Zhang, Nesreen K. Ahmed
papers.venue: Computational Linguistics, vol. 50, no. 3, pp. 1097-1179 (MIT Press / ACL)
papers.overlap: Authoritative survey of bias evaluation and mitigation in LLMs covering stereotyping, toxicity, representational harm, and distributional disparities in generated text. Explicitly cited in our §2.2 as 'gallegos2024bias' — the primary reference for the QA-era bias literature we depart from.
papers.how_ours_differs: Gallegos et al. survey bias in static LLM generation (single-turn). Our survey is organized by agent component, not by bias type. Our §2.2 explicitly states their work 'ably synthesizes' the generation literature we exclude from our scope. We differentiate by focusing on the agentic surface that their survey does not address.

papers.title: Preserving the Fairness Guarantees of Classifiers in Changing Environments: A Survey
papers.authors: A. Barrainkua, P. Gordaliza, J. A. Lozano, N. Quadrianto
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, pp. 1-31
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on fair classification under distribution shift — maintaining fairness when training and test distributions differ. Covers temporal and covariate shift. Tangentially relevant to our §3.6 (long-horizon drift) where deployment feedback loops shift data distributions.
papers.how_ours_differs: Barrainkua et al. address static classifier fairness under distributional shift. We address dynamic agent fairness under self-induced feedback loops where the agent's own outputs become future inputs (performative loops, §3.6). Their distribution-shift framing is useful background but our agentic setting has distinct mechanism and remediation needs.

papers.title: Triangular Trade-off between Robustness, Accuracy, and Fairness in Deep Neural Networks: A Survey
papers.authors: J. Li, G. Li
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, Article 140
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on the three-way trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and fairness in deep networks. Relevant as background for our §5 (mitigation) discussion of the safety-utility trade-off in debiasing.
papers.how_ours_differs: Li and Li address the trade-off in static DNN training. Our survey addresses mitigation in acting agents where the trade-off is more complex: component-level interventions can resolve a fairness problem in one pipeline stage while introducing it in another. Their three-way framing does not account for cross-component compounding (§3.7).

papers.title: Towards a Better Understanding of Evaluating Trustworthiness in AI Systems
papers.authors: Nils Kemmerzell, Annika Schreiner, Haroon Khalid, Michael Schalk, Letizia Bordoli
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, pp. 1-38
papers.overlap: CSUR survey on evaluation strategies for trustworthy AI, deriving an evaluation framework for operationalizing trustworthiness. Fairness is one of the trustworthiness dimensions evaluated. No specific LLM-agent content.
papers.how_ours_differs: Kemmerzell et al. provide a general trustworthiness evaluation framework. Our paper contributes the fairness-specific evaluation infrastructure for LLM-based agents: trajectory-level counterfactual fairness, action-level disparity metrics (CFR, MASD), and component-granular measurement — none of which are covered in their general trustworthiness framework.

papers.title: Trustworthy Information Retrieval in the LLM Era: Bias, Unfairness, and Hallucination
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Changle Qu, Sirui Chen, Xiao Zhang, Jun Xu
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference (SIGIR-AP '25)
papers.overlap: ACM SIGIR tutorial unifying bias, unfairness, and hallucination in LLM-era IR under a distribution-mismatch framework. Directly relevant to our §3.2 (retrieval bias) and §3.1 (tool selection as an IR problem).
papers.how_ours_differs: Dai et al. frame IR trustworthiness as distribution alignment within a retrieval pipeline. We situate retrieval as one of six agent components and focus on demographic fairness specifically — a narrower but deeper treatment that includes counterfactual evaluation methods and action-level disparity not present in their broader trustworthiness framing.

papers.title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Systems for Software Engineering: An SDLC-Oriented Rapid Review
papers.authors: Corey Yang-Smith, Ronnie de Souza Santos, Ahmad Abdellatif
papers.venue: arXiv preprint (April 2026), not yet ACM-published
papers.overlap: Rapid review (18 retained studies from 350) on fairness in LLM-enabled multi-agent systems for software engineering, organized by SDLC phase. Most directly overlapping our §3.3 (multi-agent delegation bias).
papers.how_ours_differs: Yang-Smith et al. scope to software engineering tasks and retain only 18 studies. Our survey covers all consequential domains (hiring, healthcare, lending, education), retains 127 papers, and provides the full six-component taxonomy with evaluation and mitigation frameworks. Their SE-specific focus is a proper subset of our coverage.

papers.title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
papers.authors: Jinhu Qi, Muzhi Li, Jiahong Liu, Yuqin Shu, Dianzhi Yu, Shicheng Ma, Wenqian Cui, and others
papers.venue: arXiv preprint 2605.23989 (May 2026)
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey on trustworthy agentic AI organized along Safety/Robustness and Privacy/System Security dimensions, decomposed by the five-stage agent lifecycle (Perceive-Plan-Act-Reflect-Learn). Notes fairness and accountability as adjacent dimensions but does not treat them.
papers.how_ours_differs: Qi et al. focus on safety, robustness, privacy, and security; fairness is explicitly listed as outside their scope. Our survey is the fairness complement to their safety/security survey. We share the agent-lifecycle anatomy but bring an entirely different measurement and mitigation apparatus for demographic disparity.

threat_assessment: No paper found substantially scoops our dedicated agent-component fairness survey. The field contains: (1) QA-era LLM fairness surveys (Gallegos et al. 2024; Chu et al. 2024) that do not cover the agentic surface; (2) general agent-evaluation surveys (Mohammadi et al. KDD '25; Yu et al. KDD '25) where fairness is one sub-topic among many with no component-organized taxonomy; (3) adjacent ACM CSUR surveys on trustworthy AI (Li et al. 2023; He et al. 2025; Ferdaus et al. 2026) that cover agent anatomy or LLM ethics but not demographic fairness organized by agent component; (4) fairness-in-ML CSUR surveys (Mehrabi 2021; Caton 2024; Pessach 2022; Tang 2023) focused on classical supervised learning. The single closest candidate is the small-scope arXiv rapid review on multi-agent SE fairness (Yang-Smith et al. 2026, 18 papers, SE domain only), which does not threaten novelty. The FAccT 2026 workshop paper (DOI 10.1145/3777490.3777504) on bias evaluation frameworks for agentic workflows is an empirical single-paper contribution, not a survey, and lacks the full taxonomy, mitigation map, and evaluation framework our survey delivers. Confirmed: as of June 2026, no dedicated, agent-component-organized, cross-domain fairness survey of LLM-based agents exists in ACM venues or arXiv. Our novelty claim ('first dedicated taxonomy of fairness in LLM-based agents organized by agent component') stands.
